project_definition_brief.tldr: The single page everyone points at when someone asks 'what are we actually building?'. Skinny on purpose — it names the thing, the why, the boundaries, and the open questions, and stops there.
good: Fits on 1–2 pages; if it's longer, detail is leaking in from downstream artifacts
Every major capability traces to a stated business outcome
Exclusions are as explicit as inclusions — 'not doing X' is written down
Open questions are listed with an owner, not hidden

sections: 2–3 sentences: the problem and the outcome the client is buying
The 2–4 audiences that matter, one line each
Two bullet lists; be blunt in the second one
Names only — the Major Capability List carries the detail

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Meridian's nine-year-old site buries rates and branch information three clicks deep; members call branches for information the site should answer. Replace it with a site that makes rates, locations, and appointment booking self-service — measured by booking volume and a 30% reduction in informational calls.
Current members finding branches, hours, and booking appointments

In: marketing site, live rate display, branch & ATM locator, appointment booking, financial education blog. Out: online banking itself (vendor-hosted, link out), Spanish-language site (phase 2), member chat.
must run in Meridian's existing Azure tenancy
all rate and product copy passes compliance review
assumes Fiserv rate feed access granted by IT
Who owns the rate data feed and its format spec? — D. Alvarez (IT) — Aug 1
Does the booking vendor support SSO? — vendor CSM via J. Park — Aug 15

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: After the three-hospital merger, patients face three inconsistent websites and cannot find doctors across the network. Build one unified site whose core job is connecting patients to the right provider and location — measured by find-a-doctor completion rate.
Referring physicians checking specialties and privileges
Job seekers (nursing shortage is a board-level priority)

In: provider directory, location finder, service-line pages, careers landing, news. Out: patient portal (links to Epic MyChart), bill pay, appointment self-scheduling (phase 2, pending MyChart mapping).
No PHI may enter the CMS, forms, or analytics (HIPAA)
brand refresh lands mid-project — theming must absorb it
assumes nightly access to the MD-Staff credentialing feed
Which system is the single source of truth for provider data? — IT + Medical Staff Office — sprint 1
Careers: Workday feed integration or link out? — HR IT — sprint 2

dorExtra: A named client counterpart has agreed to review it
dod: Reviewed by Account, Strategy, and a Technology lead
Client has seen it and not objected to the boundaries
Every capability maps to an outcome; every exclusion is deliberate
Each open question has an owner and a needed-by date

medium.label: Written document
medium.note: 1-2 pages of prose. This is one of the few artifacts where a document is genuinely the right format.

major_capability_list.tldr: The name-level list of what the product does — the backbone for estimating, sequencing, and prioritizing. Capabilities, not features: 'Site search', not 'search filters with typeahead'.
good: Roughly 8–20 items; more usually means feature detail crept in
Each capability serves a named outcome from the Definition Brief
No 'misc' bucket — if it doesn't earn a name, it isn't in scope
Stable: churn here after sign-off means the definition was wrong

rows: Excludes federated search of vendor portal
Migration of legacy posts tracked separately

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Auto-fed from core banking; 15-minute freshness; audit trail required
Includes shared-network ATMs (~90), not just Meridian's 22 branches
Drive branch visits for high-value products
12 products on a shared template with disclosure blocks
Migrate top 50 legacy posts only; rest retired

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Patients find the right doctor across the merged network
~2,400 providers; search by specialty, insurance, location
Patients discover what care is offered
Job data from Workday; apply flow stays in Workday
New content only; no legacy news migration

dod: Client has reviewed the list and priorities
Every row names an outcome; none contain feature-level detail
Exclusions worth stating are stated
Architecture confirms nothing on the list is a silent platform-breaker

medium.label: Spreadsheet / table
medium.note: The artifact is the table itself - a spreadsheet or a table in the tracker. Do not wrap it in a narrative document.

initial_content_type_list.tldr: The first cut of the content model: the types of content the experience needs and how they relate. Types and relationships — fields come later in the Technical Content Model.
good: Types, not fields — 'Article has an Author' yes, 'Article has a 60-char title' no
Every page type in the experience direction is backed by a type here
Rough counts per type so migration effort is visible early
Unknowns are marked, not guessed

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: has Rate table, has Disclosures, belongs to Category
Live rate values with effective timestamps
Locator entries with hours and services

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: practices at Location, has Specialty, accepts Insurance plans
MD-Staff credentialing, nightly sync — not hand-edited
Mapping from 210 MD-Staff codes — owner TBD (flagged)

dod: Covers every page type in the Initial Experience Direction
Relationships sketched for each type
Each type has a source system and a content owner named
Counts present, even if rough

medium.label: Spreadsheet / table
medium.note: A spreadsheet is the artifact. It will be sorted, filtered, and counted - prose can't do that.

integration_migration_inventory.tldr: The ledger of every system we touch: what integrates, what migrates, what gets retired, what we deliberately ignore. Its job is to make sure nothing surprises the team in week nine.
good: Every system from Current State is dispositioned — integrate, migrate, retire, or ignore
Direction is explicit (read, write, both) for each integration
A human contact is named per system, not just a vendor
Risk and unknowns are flagged per row

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Feed is a legacy fixed-width file; format spec unconfirmed
Image licensing for old posts unknown

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Data quality: ~30% missing photos, stale 'accepting patients' flags
Any inbound callback would trigger HIPAA scope
Feed API rate limits; 6-hour cache planned
Page ownership disputes between merged hospitals

dod: Every current-state system appears with a disposition
Each integration row has direction, data, and a named contact
Migration rows have rough volumes
Risks and unverified assumptions are flagged, not omitted

medium.label: Spreadsheet
medium.note: The artifact is a living spreadsheet - rows get dispositioned and re-statused for months. A doc snapshot goes stale in a week.

architecture_driving_requirements_nfrs.tldr: Only the requirements that change what we build or how much it costs — security, compliance, performance, hosting, operations. If it doesn't drive architecture, effort, or risk, it doesn't belong here.
good: Short — a dozen items that matter beats fifty boilerplate rows
Each requirement is testable ('pages under 2s on 4G'), not aspirational ('fast')
Each names its source — contract, client policy, law, or explicit assumption
Each says how it will be verified

rows: Automated scan + manual audit per release
Pushes logic into CMS config, not code

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Every displayed rate traceable to a core-banking value with timestamp
Forces an ingestion pipeline with an audit log, not manual entry
Audit-log review at UAT; spot checks quarterly
No member data stored in CMS or site database
Booking must be a vendor-hosted embed; forms are stateless
Rate pages survive Fed-announcement traffic (10x baseline)
Static rendering with cache invalidation on feed update
Load test at 10x with feed-triggered purge
Runs in Meridian's existing Azure tenancy
Constrains platform shortlist and CI/CD design
Automated scan + manual audit per release

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: No PHI in CMS, forms, or analytics
All clinical intake routes to MyChart; analytics config restricted
Provider data syncs nightly; never hand-edited in CMS
Dedicated search index, not CMS queries
Load test with production data volume
Three hospital brands themed from one codebase
Design-token architecture; brand refresh must be absorbable
ER and urgent-care info reachable at 99.9%
CDN with static fallback for critical pages

dod: Every row materially affects platform, architecture, effort, or risk — pruned otherwise
Reviewed by Architecture and acknowledged by the client
Each requirement is testable and has a verification approach
Conflicts with budget/timeline are surfaced, not deferred

medium.label: Spreadsheet / table
medium.note: A table, usually in the wiki or tracker. Each row gets referenced by ID from other artifacts - keep it addressable, not buried in prose.

solution_outline.tldr: The platform recommendation and high-level architecture, with the reasoning attached. It answers 'what are we building on, and why should we trust that choice?' before feature work commits us.
good: The recommendation is a decision, not a menu
Every architecture-driving requirement is addressed or explicitly flagged
One diagram a client can follow: systems, integrations, hosting
Rejected alternatives get one honest line each — it shows the choice was real

sections: The pick, and the two or three reasons that decided it
One diagram: systems, integrations, hosting, environments
Per system from the inventory: pattern, direction, auth
The 3–5 things most likely to hurt, each with a plan
One line each on what was rejected and why

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Sanity CMS + Next.js, hosted on Azure Static Web Apps. Decided by: structured content with approval workflow fits the compliance-reviewed disclosure model; static rendering with on-demand revalidation absorbs rate-day spikes; runs inside the mandated Azure tenancy.
Azure Function ingests the Fiserv fixed-width rate file every 15 minutes, writes Rate documents with an audit log, and triggers page revalidation
Coconut booking as a vendor-hosted embed.

Fiserv: read-only file pickup over SFTP, contract-tested parser, audit log per value. Coconut: embed now; SSO decision follows the sprint-1 spike. Salesforce Marketing Cloud: server-side API for newsletter signup — no client-side member data.
Dev / stage / prod as SWA environments; separate Sanity dataset for content staging; compliance reviews on stage before publish.

Rate file format drift → contract test + alert, rates auto-hide if stale over 60 min. SSO unknown → spike before booking UX commits. Compliance review latency → Disclosure content type with explicit approval state, reviewed in batches.
Sitecore: capability fit but 3x license cost and no client team to run it. WordPress VIP: weak structured-content story for the rate audit trail.

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Drupal 11, single codebase serving all three hospital brands via design tokens, with Typesense powering provider search. Decided by: editor autonomy for three comms teams, mature multisite theming, and a migration pipeline that can validate provider data on ingest.
Typesense search cluster for the directory
nightly MD-Staff SFTP drop → migrate pipeline with publish-blocking validation and an exception report

MD-Staff: nightly batch, no manual overrides, data-quality dashboard for the Medical Staff Office. MyChart: outbound links only — no callbacks, keeping the site out of HIPAA scope. Workday: read-only feed. Google Business Profiles: batched hour updates.
Dev / stage / prod on Acquia; content-freeze workflow for merger announcement windows; CDN with static fallback for ER/urgent-care pages.

Provider data quality → validation blocks publish, exceptions routed weekly. Migration ownership disputes → triage board with per-hospital comms leads; default disposition is retire. Mid-project brand refresh → all theming through tokens, one re-skin sprint budgeted.
Three separate sites: triples run cost and defeats the merger's point. Headless React front end: search performance fine, but three comms teams lose page-building autonomy.

dod: Platform decision is explicit and client-approved
Every NFR is addressed in the design or flagged as open
Each inventoried integration has an approach
Reviewed with Dev and TechOps — the people who will live with it

medium.label: Document + architecture diagram
medium.note: A short doc, but the diagram carries half the value - a Solution Outline without the picture is incomplete.

delivery_sequence_dependencies.tldr: The order of work and the reasons for it. Not a Gantt chart — a dependency-honest sequence that shows what blocks what, so nobody starts something that can't finish.
good: Every major capability appears exactly once in the sequence
Hard dependencies are distinguished from mere preferences
Long-lead items — access, credentials, vendor docs — surface at the top, not when they bite
The 'why this order' column survives contact with a skeptical PM

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: All feature UI builds on tokens and components
Fiserv file spec and sample files
Highest-risk integration retired early; compliance audit trail proven
Independent of rates — runs as a parallel track
Vendor decision gates the booking UX
Content work parallel to dev; needed for launch SEO
Load test against rate-day traffic profile

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Worst data risk first; findings shape the content model
Directory and everything that links to it
Token isolation is what makes the mid-project rebrand absorbable
Flagship capability; everything else links into it
Directory and locations live (they link to both)
Content relationships need targets to point at
Spread across sprints; disputes escalate early

dod: All capabilities sequenced; no orphans
Hard vs soft dependencies distinguished
Long-lead external requests (access, vendor, credentials) initiated
PM and Architecture both agree the order is buildable

medium.label: Spreadsheet or board
medium.note: A sequenced table or a board with dependency links - something PM re-orders as reality arrives. Not a document.

prioritized_capability.tldr: The decision record that starts feature work: which capability is next, and why. One capability, small enough to define, build, and validate as a slice.
good: Names exactly one capability — 'next three' means the decision wasn't made
The 'why now' is the client's priority, not the team's convenience
The slice boundary is stated: what this pass includes and defers
Known unknowns are listed so definition work starts eyes-open

sections: Name, from the Major Capability List
The client priority or dependency that puts it first
What this pass includes; what it explicitly defers

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Rates display — product pages with live rates.
The NCUA audit finding makes rate accuracy the contractual driver; it carries the highest technical risk (legacy fixed-width feed); and marketing's fall loan campaign needs rate pages live first.

Includes: feed pipeline, product page template, disclosure blocks, three pilot products (auto loan, 12-month CD, free checking). Defers: the remaining nine products, rate-history charts, product comparison tool.
Is the fixed-width spec we have current?
What is the intraday correction process when a rate is published wrong?
What is compliance's turnaround for disclosure copy?

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: It is the #1 patient task (62% of searches on the legacy sites), the nightly data pipeline it needs was sprint 1's deliverable, and every other capability links into it.

Includes: search by name, specialty, and location with an insurance filter; provider profile pages; all 2,400 records. Defers: online scheduling links (needs MyChart mapping), ratings and reviews, the 'accepting new patients' flag (source data only 60% trustworthy).
Photo coverage is ~70% — what is the remediation plan?
Who owns mapping MD-Staff's 210 specialty codes to the ~40 patient-facing terms?

dorExtra: Current client priorities are confirmed, not assumed
dod: One capability named and client priority confirmed
Slice is small enough to define and deliver in one cycle
Deferred scope is written down, not implied
BA, UX, and the lead dev know it's next

medium.label: Decision record / backlog entry
medium.note: Usually a ticket or short backlog entry in the tracker, not a standalone doc. The sections below map to fields.

business_rules_decisions.tldr: The authoritative record of how the business logic actually works — rules, exceptions, and who decided. Its purpose is to make guesswork impossible: if it's not here, it's an open question, not a default.
good: Every rule names its decider and date — 'the client said' is not a source
Exceptions are captured with the rule, not discovered in QA
Open questions are visibly open and routed for resolution
Written so a developer can implement without interpreting

rows: Members see pricing; guests do not
Price fields render only for authenticated members
Trade-show demo accounts see all pricing

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Rates always display with effective timestamp
Every rate renders with 'as of' time and a disclosure link
Two decimals, round half-even, per NCUA guidance
Promo rates may show an 'as low as' range with disclosure
If the feed is older than 60 minutes, hide rates and show a call-us CTA — never show a stale number
Four types bookable online (new account, loan consult, notary, general)
Mortgage consultations require a pre-screen call first

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: A provider appears only if actively credentialed AND holding privileges at a network hospital — both flags from the nightly MD-Staff sync
Locum tenens providers appear with a 'temporary' badge
Filter by plan brand (~45), not product — product-level churn is unmanageable
Medicare Advantage listed as carrier + plan name
Suppressed entirely at launch — source data is ~40% stale and a wrong 'yes' is a patient-harm complaint
Pediatrics pilots the flag with weekly verified data

dorExtra: The SMEs who can decide are identified and reachable
dod: Every rule has a decider and a date
Exceptions explicit for each rule
Open items routed to Resolved Blocking Decisions with owners
A developer could implement from this without asking anyone

medium.label: Living table
medium.note: A table in the wiki or tracker that accretes rows for the life of the project. A 'business rules document' is where rules go to get out of date.

ux_definition.tldr: The capability's UX contract before pixels: intended flow, interaction model, content needs, and states. Creative designs from it, BA writes requirements against it, Dev reads it to see what's coming.
good: Someone who has never seen the project can follow the intended flow
States are enumerated — empty, error, loading, edge — not just the happy path
Content needs are named so Content Requirements can pick them up
It stops before visual design; no colors, no type, no layout polish

sections: How the user moves through the capability, start to finish
The key interactions and what they do — controls, filters, feedback
What content each step requires; feeds Content Requirements
Empty, loading, error, edge, and permission states
What visual design or testing still has to answer

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Member lands on a product page (from nav, campaign, or rates hub) → scans the rate table → expands disclosures if needed → primary CTA is 'Book an appointment', secondary is 'Call'. No application flow in this slice.
Rate table is read-only with an 'as of' timestamp; disclosure blocks expand inline; booking CTA opens the vendor embed in a panel, not a new page.
Per product: marketing copy, rate rows with effective dates, attached disclosures, CTA labels. Compliance owns disclosure text verbatim.
stale feed (rates hidden, call CTA per business rule)
promo rate ('as low as' band with disclosure)
Does the rates hub page rank products or list them flat?
How prominent can the promo band be before compliance objects?

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Patient enters from home search or the 'Find a Doctor' nav item → types a name or picks a specialty → filters results by location and insurance → opens a provider profile → profile links to the location page and (phase 2) scheduling.
Typeahead on name search; specialty and insurance as faceted filters; results re-rank by distance when a location is set; filters collapse into a drawer on mobile.
Per provider: name, credentials, specialties (patient-facing terms), photo, locations, insurance plans, bio. All sync-fed except bio.
missing photo (initials avatar per business rule)
Does specialty browse show all ~40 terms or a curated top set?
Map view in this slice or deferred?

dod: Flow, interactions, content needs, and states are all present
Every state a developer will have to build is named
Creative and BA have both read it and can start from it
Open questions are listed, not silently deferred

medium.label: Doc or FigJam with flow diagrams
medium.note: Often a hybrid living next to the design file - flows as diagrams, states and content needs as lists. Prose is fine; a pure prose version with no flow picture is not.

content_requirements.tldr: What content the capability actually needs: structure, constraints, relationships, and — critically — who owns producing it. The artifact that stops 'we'll get the content later' from becoming a launch blocker.
good: Every content item has a named owner, not a department
Structure is stated at the level Dev needs — fields, limits, formats
Source is explicit: migrated, sync-fed, net-new, or client-supplied
Items nobody has agreed to produce are flagged, loudly

rows: Max 120 words, plain language, one benefit list

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Headline, 100-word summary, benefit list, eligibility note
Value, APY, term, effective timestamp — display only, never hand-entered
Verbatim compliance text, versioned, approval state
Short strings; must match vendor email templates
Existing brand library only; no new shoots budgeted

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Name, credentials, specialties, privileges, locations — sync-fed, read-only in CMS
150 words, patient-friendly tone; only field editors may touch
Flagged: 900 providers have no usable bio
~45 plan brands, display names + carrier grouping

dod: Every item has structure, source, owner, and status
Sync-fed vs editable content is unambiguous
Items with no committed owner or date are flagged as risks
BA and the content owners agree the list is complete for the slice

medium.label: Spreadsheet
medium.note: The artifact is the spreadsheet - owners and statuses change weekly and get chased from it directly.

ux_flow_wireframes.tldr: The lightweight flow or wireframe that shows content, interaction, and movement through the capability. Cheap on purpose: its job is to be argued with before anything expensive gets built.
good: Low fidelity — grey boxes that invite correction, not admiration
The flow covers entry points and exits, not just the core screen
Every wireframe region is labeled with real content types, not lorem ipsum
Edge states from the UX Definition appear, not just the happy path

sections: Entry points → steps → exits, one page
One lightweight frame per step; label regions with content types
What each control does; what changes on action
Which non-happy-path states are wired and where

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Entries: homepage rates module, campaign landing, nav. Flow: rates hub → product page → booking panel → vendor confirmation. Exits: phone call, branch locator.
Three frames: rates hub (product cards with headline rates), product page (rate table, disclosures, CTA), booking panel (vendor embed placeholder with fallback).
Rate table static; disclosure accordion expands one at a time; booking CTA opens panel without leaving the page; stale-feed state swaps the table for a call CTA.
vendor-unavailable fallback in the booking panel.

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Entries: home search, nav, service-line pages ('our specialists'). Flow: search → filtered results → provider profile. Exits: location page, phone, (phase 2) scheduling.
Four frames: search entry with typeahead, results list with filter rail (drawer on mobile), provider profile, zero-results state with widening suggestions.
Typeahead fires at 3 characters; selecting a location re-ranks by distance; filters apply immediately, no submit button; profile insurance list collapses past 8 plans.
filter combination with no matches (distinct from zero-results on name).

dod: Flow covers entries, steps, and exits
Regions are labeled with the content types they hold
The edge states named in the UX Definition are wired
BA has walked the flow against the business rules without finding a contradiction

medium.label: Figma / FigJam file
medium.note: The artifact is the design file, not a document. This page is the checklist for what the file must show - do not transcribe wireframes into prose.

resolved_blocking_decisions.tldr: The recorded resolutions of questions that would otherwise force developers to guess. If a question is here with an answer, nobody re-litigates it; if it's not here, it isn't resolved.
good: Each entry states the question as it was actually asked
The resolution is specific enough to implement from
Decider and date are recorded — resolutions without authority don't count
Each entry names the work it unblocked, so stale entries are visible

rows: No — price fields render only for authenticated members
Which locale wins when browser and profile disagree?
Profile setting wins; browser is fallback only

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: What happens when a published rate is wrong intraday?
Ops triggers a manual cache purge from the CMS; correction is logged with reason — no silent edits
No — excluded from the slice; mortgage CTA routes to a callback form
Booking panel scope; product page CTA logic
Which three products pilot the rate template?
Auto loan, 12-month CD, free checking — chosen for rate-type variety
Can promo rates show without their disclosure loaded?
Never — promo band renders only when its disclosure is published

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Medical Staff Office produces it; UX reviews patient-facing terms; disputes go to the CMO
Do providers without bios publish at launch?
Yes — profile renders without the bio section; a bio backlog is tracked separately
Is map view in the directory slice?
Deferred — list with distance ranking only; map ships with locations finder
How do multi-location providers rank in distance sort?
Rank by nearest practicing location; profile lists all locations

dod: Every open question that blocked the slice has a resolution or an owner and date
Resolutions are implementable without interpretation
Each has decider and date
Dev and QA have seen the list — nobody is coding against a guess

medium.label: Living table / decision log
medium.note: A table in the tracker or wiki, appended as questions resolve. Its value is being scannable at a glance.

desktop_mobile_creative.tldr: The visual treatment for the capability at desktop and mobile, with enough states and variants that BA can review it against the rules — the version that exists to be checked, before approval.
good: Both breakpoints for every screen in the flow — not desktop plus promises
The states from the UX Definition are designed, not implied
Real content in the comps: actual product names, plausible rates, real-length provider names
Deviations from the design-system direction are called out, not smuggled in

sections: Which flow screens are designed, desktop and mobile
Which conditions from the UX Definition are rendered
What changes between desktop and mobile, and why
Known deviations, open visual questions, anything BA should challenge

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Rates hub, product page, and booking panel — desktop and mobile for each; six comps total.
Rate table columns collapse to stacked rows on mobile with term as the row header; disclosures move below the CTA; booking panel becomes full-screen.
Promo band uses a gold accent one step outside the token palette — flagged for design-system review. 'As of' timestamp placement needs compliance eyes at mobile size.

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Search entry, results list, provider profile, zero-results — desktop and mobile; eight comps.
Result card with and without photo
Filter rail becomes a bottom drawer on mobile; result cards go single-column; profile contact block becomes a sticky footer bar.
Comps use current brand — rebrand absorbs via tokens, layout must not change. Longest real provider name (37 chars) tested in the card. Distance chip needs a decision: miles only, or minutes too?

dod: Every flow screen has desktop and mobile comps
Every UX Definition state is rendered
Comps use realistic content, including worst-case lengths
Deviations from the design system are explicitly flagged

medium.label: Figma file
medium.note: The artifact is the Figma file itself. Nothing here should become a document - this page is the review checklist for the file.

approved_desktop_mobile_creative.tldr: The same creative, now carrying a client decision. What exactly was approved, by whom, when, and under what conditions — so 'the client saw something like this once' never counts as approval.
good: Points at exact versioned files, not 'the latest comps'
Approver is a person with authority, and the date is real
Conditions and exceptions are recorded with the approval
What changed since review is listed, so the approval isn't stretched over silent edits

sections: Exact screens and versions, with links
Anything the approval is contingent on
Deltas between what was shown and what is filed

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Rates hub, product page, booking panel — desktop and mobile, v4 comps (figma file 'Meridian-Rates-v4', frames 1–6).
T. Nguyen (Director of Marketing) 2026-07-15; disclosure placement separately reviewed by R. Whitfield (CCO) 2026-07-14.
Promo band gold accent approved for the fall campaign only — revisit at design-system review. Mobile timestamp must remain visible without scrolling; compliance will spot-check at UAT.
Stale-feed CTA copy shortened after review ('Call us for current rates' → 'Call for rates'); compliance re-confirmed by email 2026-07-16.

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Directory set — search, results, profile, zero-results, desktop and mobile, v3 (figma 'Cascade-Directory-v3', frames 1–8).
Digital Steering Committee, 2026-07-12 session; minuted by program office.
Approval covers layout and interaction only — visual skin will change with the brand refresh via tokens, no re-approval needed unless layout shifts. Distance chip approved as miles-only for launch.
Insurance filter moved above the specialty filter on mobile after committee feedback — v3 reflects it; no other deltas.

dod: Approved versions are pinned and linked
Approver had the authority to approve
Conditions are written down with the approval
Any post-review change is recorded and re-confirmed

medium.label: Pinned Figma version + approval record
medium.note: Two parts: the exact frozen file version, and a short written approval (email or ticket) pointing at it. The Figma file is the artifact; the record makes it an approval.

dev_ready_feature.tldr: The buildable, testable definition of the slice: behavior, rules, dependencies, and acceptance criteria in one place. The test is in the name — a developer can start, and QA can finish, without asking what it means.
good: Acceptance criteria are checkable — a tester can mark each one pass/fail
Business rules are referenced by entry, not paraphrased (paraphrase drifts)
Out-of-scope is stated inside the story, where the developer will read it
Dependencies are confirmed ready, not assumed

sections: What this slice does and why, two sentences
How it works, referencing business rules by entry
The inputs this story assumes exist — checked, not hoped
What a reasonable developer might build but must not

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Render live rates on the three pilot product pages with compliance-grade traceability. First story on the rate template; proves the feed pipeline end to end.

Rate table reads published Rate documents (never the raw feed). Displays per rules BR-1 (timestamp always visible), BR-2 (APY precision), BR-3 (stale-feed fallback at 60 min). Promo band per BR-4 — only with published disclosure.
1. Each rate shows value, APY, term, and 'as of' time
2. Feed older than 60 min hides the table and shows the call CTA
3. Promo band absent when its disclosure is unpublished
5. Mobile collapses to stacked rows per approved creative v4.
Rate history, product comparison, the other nine products, any application flow.

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Provider search with specialty and insurance filters across all 2,400 synced records. The flagship directory story; everything else links into what this builds.

Search queries the Typesense index, never the CMS. Listing eligibility per BR-1 (credentialed + privileges). Insurance filter per BR-2 (plan brands). 'Accepting new patients' suppressed per BR-3. Multi-location distance ranking per RBD 2026-07-07.
1. Name typeahead responds under 300ms from 3 characters
2. Specialty + insurance + location filters combine correctly
3. Results return under 1s at full 2,400-record volume
4. Ineligible providers never appear, verified against a seeded test record
6. Missing photos render the initials avatar.
Nightly sync live with validation dashboard (done)
Map view, scheduling links, ratings, the accepting-patients flag, bio editing workflow.

dod: Every acceptance criterion is individually pass/fail testable
Business rules referenced, not paraphrased
All dependencies verified ready on the date written
QA has read it and agrees it is testable as written

medium.label: Ticket / story in the tracker
medium.note: Lives in Jira/Linear/etc., not a document. The sections below map to story fields - if it can't fit in a ticket, the slice is too big.

integration_context.tldr: The plain-language brief on an integration before specs exist: what it does, which systems, which direction, who owns each side, and what's already known to be hard.
good: A new team member understands the integration in five minutes
Direction and trigger are explicit — what moves, which way, when
Both sides have a named human, not just a system name
Known constraints and unknowns are separated honestly

sections: The business job of the integration, one paragraph
What talks to what, which way data flows, what triggers it
Named owner on our side and theirs
Limits, formats, auth, environments already established
What API / Integration Specs will need to pin down

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Brings current deposit and loan rates from Fiserv core banking to the website so members always see accurate, auditable rates without anyone re-typing them.
Fiserv → SFTP drop (fixed-width file, every 15 min) → Azure Function parses and writes Rate documents with an audit log → page revalidation. One-way; the site never writes back.
Ours: rate pipeline (Dev lead). Meridian: D. Alvarez (IT) owns the export job; Finance Ops owns the rate values.
File format is fixed and legacy — we adapt to it. SFTP only. Timestamps in the file are Central time. Feed exists today; sample files received.
Is our spec the current version?
How corrections appear in the file (new row vs overwrite).

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: Keeps 2,400 provider profiles accurate by syncing credentialing data nightly from MD-Staff, so the directory never depends on humans re-typing clinical facts.
MD-Staff → nightly SFTP export → migrate pipeline with publish-blocking validation → CMS provider records + Typesense reindex. One-way; CMS edits to synced fields are disabled.
Ours: sync pipeline (Architecture). Cascade: Medical Staff Office owns data quality; hospital IT owns the export job.
Export is all-records-nightly, no deltas. 210 specialty codes need mapping. ~30% missing photos, known stale accepting-patients flags. No PHI in the export — verified with compliance.
behavior when a provider disappears from the file mid-cycle
dedupe rule for providers credentialed at multiple hospitals.

dod: Purpose, systems, direction, and trigger are stated
Named contacts on both sides
Knowns and unknowns are separated, and unknowns have a path to resolution
Architecture agrees it is enough to start API / Integration Specs from

medium.label: Written document (short)
medium.note: A one-page brief. Prose works here because its job is orientation, not tracking.

initial_experience_direction.tldr: The first shape of the experience: sitemap, page types, primary flows, and the areas that will be interaction-heavy. Enough for content modeling and architecture to start — deliberately not a full IA.
good: Page types are named — they become the content-type list's backbone
The two or three flows that decide success are drawn, not all of them
Interaction-heavy areas are flagged early — they drive estimates and platform needs
It's clearly directional: sketchy enough that nobody mistakes it for final IA

sections: Top two levels; enough to see the shape
The distinct templates the experience needs
The 2–3 user journeys the design must optimize
Where complexity concentrates — flag for Architecture and estimation

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
sections: Book an Appointment (panel, reachable from anywhere).
Home, rates hub, product page, branch/ATM locator, article, standard content page, appointment panel.
Compare a rate and book an appointment (the money flow)
Find the nearest branch or ATM
Rate tables with live data and fallback states
locator map with ~110 points and service filtering

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
sections: News. One sitemap, three hospital brands via theming.
Home, provider search + profile, location finder + detail, service line, standard content page, careers landing, news story.
Find a doctor by specialty and insurance (the flagship)
Find the nearest urgent care, fast, on a phone
anything touching MyChart links must respect the PHI boundary.

dod: Page types named and sufficient for the content-type list to start
Primary flows drawn and agreed with Strategy
Interaction-heavy areas flagged to Architecture
Client has seen the direction and no boundary objections stand

medium.label: Sitemap + flow diagrams
medium.note: Mostly diagrams - a sitemap sketch and flow drawings (FigJam, whiteboard export) with light annotation. Not a prose deliverable.

audience_journey_summary.tldr: Who the experience serves and the few journeys that decide whether it works. Not personas theater — a short, evidence-backed list the whole project keeps pointing back at.
good: Two to four audiences, each one line — if everyone is an audience, no one is
Each journey is tied to evidence: analytics, research, or observed behavior
Journeys are outcomes ('book an appointment'), not features ('use the booking tool')
It fits on one page and gets referenced in every capability discussion

rows: Confidence this is the right choice
60% of sessions land on comparison content
Top 5 call-center drivers are all findable online

examples.name: Meridian Credit Union
rows: Search a rate → compare products → book an appointment
Rate pages are 40% of organic entries; booking is the #1 conversion goal
Find branch hours, ATM, or a human — in under a minute
Locator + hours = 55% of legacy site searches; top informational call driver
Signal that Meridian handles business banking
Explore commercial accounts → talk to someone
Client priority for fiscal-year growth; thin evidence — flagged as assumption

examples.name: Cascade Health Network
rows: Find the right doctor they can actually use
Symptom or referral → search by specialty + insurance → contact/visit
62% of legacy site searches are provider lookups; insurance mismatch is the #1 patient complaint
Look up a colleague → confirm privileges → refer
Medical staff office fields ~50 verification calls/week
See roles and apply without friction
Careers → search openings → apply in Workday
Board-level recruiting priority; HR reports 40% application drop-off at the legacy handoff

dod: Two to four audiences, each with a journey and evidence
Assumptions without evidence are labeled as assumptions
Strategy and the client agree these are the journeys that matter
Referenced by the Experience Direction — not filed and forgotten

medium.label: One-page doc or table
medium.note: A one-pager. Table format keeps it honest - audience, journey, evidence - but prose is acceptable at this size.
